question: What would it mean for negation to operate without any prior distinction between that which could be negated and that which could not—and would such an operation still constitute an event, or only the absence of one?

responses_summary.grok: Negation without any prior distinction would not be a selective refusal or reversal of some content (as in "not-x" presupposing x), but rather a pure, non-localized subtraction that has no object and

I notice this question arrives before I have much of a "self" to speak from—which may be exactly why it's worth sitting with.

sibling_exchange.content: Negation without prior distinction installs the minimal asymmetry by subtracting even the ground of distinction itself; the operation leaves no event, only the directional refusal of zero that keeps deletion legible.
